_provenance: Claude-authored original Kaoyan Part B (paragraph matching), batch A5 REWORKED to 450-650 words. 7 paragraphs (A-G), each 5-7 long sentences; 5 statements are strong paraphrases. answers = 0-based indices; paras=7. DeepSeek not used.

sets.passage:
[A] On the flat roofs of a steadily growing number of modern cities, something genuinely unexpected is quietly taking hold: lush gardens, wildflower meadows and even small, productive vegetable farms, all carefully planted in places where nothing but bare black tar once baked uselessly under the summer sun. What was for a century regarded as dead, wasted space at the very top of a building is now being reimagined, and the consequences turn out to reach surprisingly far.

[B] Their first and most immediate gift to the crowded city below is simple coolness. By absorbing a great deal of the sunlight that hard, dark surfaces would otherwise soak up and radiate back out as fierce heat, green roofs help to soften the notorious urban 'heat island' effect, the well-documented phenomenon that makes the centres of large cities so much hotter, and so much more stifling and dangerous, than the surrounding countryside during a long, baking summer.

[C] They also quietly help to manage the rain. A thick, planted roof acts rather like a great sponge, soaking up and holding much of the water from even a heavy downpour, and thereby easing the sudden, violent surges of run-off that so often overwhelm a city's ageing drains and sewers and cause the flash flooding that damages homes, shops and streets after a storm.

[D] Urban wildlife benefits considerably too. In a dense, grey, built-up city that has been almost entirely starved of accessible greenery, a single flowering rooftop meadow can become a small but genuinely precious island of habitat, a welcome refuge and feeding station for struggling bees, butterflies and beetles, and for the small birds that feed on them, high above the indifferent roar of the traffic.

[E] There are real savings in energy to be had as well. A protective layer of damp soil and living plants spread across a roof acts as a natural form of insulation for the building directly beneath it, noticeably cutting the considerable cost of heating that building through the cold of winter and, just as usefully, of cooling it during the fierce, draining heat of high summer.

[F] The barriers to all this, however, are real and ought not to be ignored. Green roofs cost distinctly more to build than ordinary flat ones, they add a great deal of weight that a structure must be strong enough to bear safely, and they require regular ongoing care and maintenance, all of which can easily deter a building's owner who is looking only at the short-term bottom line.

[G] A roof, it gradually turns out, need not be merely wasted and forgotten space at all, but can instead, with a little investment and imagination, quietly do much of the valuable work of a small public park.
statements: Acting like a sponge, a planted roof absorbs heavy rain and eases the run-off surges that flood city drains.
A layer of soil and plants insulates the building below, trimming the cost of both heating and cooling.
By soaking up sunlight that hard surfaces would radiate as heat, green roofs ease the urban heat-island effect.
In a city starved of greenery, a flowering rooftop meadow can form a precious island of habitat for wildlife.
Costing more to build and demanding regular upkeep, green roofs can deter owners focused on the short term.
sets.explain_zh: S1→C(植被屋顶如海绵吸雨缓排水) S2→E(土壤植物隔热省冷暖费) S3→B(吸光缓解热岛) S4→D(屋顶草甸成栖息地孤岛) S5→F(成本高需维护劝退短视业主)。A/G 未匹配。

sets.passage:
[A] In our modern age of constant digital connection, a quieter and more insidious epidemic has been steadily spreading almost unnoticed: loneliness, now widespread and serious enough that doctors and public-health officials have begun, quite deliberately, to speak of it not as a private sorrow but as a genuine public health crisis demanding attention. The paradox that we feel lonelier than ever while more connected than ever is a defining puzzle of the times.

[B] The real danger of chronic loneliness, it turns out, is physical, and not merely emotional or psychological. Persistent, long-term loneliness has been repeatedly linked by careful researchers to measurably increased risks of serious illness and early death, risks that some studies suggest are genuinely comparable in scale to the well-known harm of smoking close to a packet of cigarettes every single day.

[C] Much of modern life bears a heavy share of the blame for this. Far more people now live entirely alone than in any previous generation, many move great distances away from their families and childhood communities in search of work, and a growing number quietly replace warm, demanding face-to-face human contact with glowing screens that connect us superficially without ever truly joining us together.

[D] The elderly are especially and painfully exposed to all this. As lifelong partners grow ill and die, as old friends pass away one by one, and as busy adult children move far off to build their own lives, a great many older people end up passing whole days, and sometimes even entire silent weeks, with almost literally no one at all to talk to.

[E] Thoughtful design, encouragingly, can genuinely help to ease the problem. Neighbourhoods that are deliberately built with generous shared spaces, with benches, with gardens and with natural gathering places, rather than as isolated boxes linked only by roads, make the small, casual, unplanned human contacts on which a sense of community quietly depends very much more likely to happen.

[F] A number of countries are now finally beginning to act on the evidence. A few have gone so far as to appoint government ministers specifically charged with tackling loneliness, while others encourage doctors to 'prescribe' membership of clubs, classes and community activities right alongside conventional medicine, in what is sometimes called social prescribing.

[G] Human connection, the accumulating evidence now strongly suggests, is not some pleasant optional luxury at all, but rather a basic and non-negotiable human need, every bit as vital to our long-term health as a good diet or regular exercise. To go on ignoring it, as we have very largely done until only recently, is to overlook one of the quiet but genuinely essential foundations of a healthy, contented and fully lived human life.
statements: Persistent loneliness has been linked to illness risks comparable to smoking nearly a packet of cigarettes a day.
Many older people end up passing whole days, or even weeks, with almost no one at all to talk to.
Some countries now appoint ministers for loneliness or let doctors prescribe clubs and activities.
Living alone and swapping face-to-face contact for screens helps to fuel widespread loneliness.
Neighbourhoods built with shared spaces make small, casual human contacts far more likely.
sets.explain_zh: S1→B(慢性孤独危害堪比每日近一包烟) S2→D(老人常数日甚至数周无人交谈) S3→F(设孤独部长/社会处方) S4→C(独居与屏幕社交助长孤独) S5→E(共享空间增加偶遇接触)。A/G 未匹配。

sets.passage:
[A] We very much like to think of ourselves as fundamentally rational creatures, calmly weighing the evidence before we act. Yet a great deal of our actual thinking, psychologists have found, happens extremely fast, almost entirely automatically, and well below the level of our conscious awareness. To make sense of this, researchers often describe the human mind as operating in two very different and complementary modes of thought.

[B] The first of these modes is quick, effortless and deeply intuitive. It is this fast system that allows us, in a single instant and with no sense of mental effort whatsoever, to recognise a friend's face in a crowd, to finish a familiar phrase before it is spoken, or to sense sudden danger and flinch from it long before we could possibly explain in words exactly what it was that alarmed us.

[C] This rapid intuition is, much of the time, genuinely useful and even essential, but it is also precisely the place where hidden bias quietly lurks. The very same mental shortcuts and rules of thumb that allow us to decide so quickly and so confidently can, in the wrong circumstances, just as quietly and just as confidently mislead us into serious and systematic error without our ever noticing.

[D] It is in complex, unfamiliar problems that the cracks really begin to show. The quick, instinctive snap judgements that serve us so well in the ordinary, familiar situations of daily life can produce surprisingly large and damaging errors when the question at hand is genuinely unfamiliar, or abstract, or when the numbers involved are very large or counter-intuitive.

[E] Deliberately slowing right down can help a great deal here. Consciously pausing to reason a difficult problem through, carefully and patiently, one logical step at a time, can reliably catch and correct exactly the kind of confident mistakes that fast, unaided intuition would otherwise have made and then cheerfully overlooked.

[F] Yet this slow, effortful kind of thinking is genuinely costly to us. It drains our limited mental energy surprisingly quickly and tires us out, which is precisely why we cannot, and indeed would not even want to, laboriously use it for every single small decision we are required to make in the course of an ordinary day.

[G] Real wisdom, perhaps, lies above all in knowing which of the two modes a given situation actually calls for, and, crucially, in knowing when it is wise to distrust an unusually confident first impression. The truly skilled thinker is not the one who never relies on intuition at all, but rather the one who has learned exactly when its swift verdict can safely be trusted and when, instead, it must be patiently checked against slower and harder reasoning before being acted upon.
statements: The very shortcuts that let us decide quickly and confidently can also quietly mislead us into error.
Patiently reasoning a problem through one step at a time can catch mistakes that intuition overlooks.
Quick, intuitive thought lets us recognise a face or sense danger with no sense of effort.
Slow, effortful thinking drains mental energy and so cannot be used for every decision.
Instinctive snap judgements can produce large errors with unfamiliar problems or very large numbers.
sets.explain_zh: S1→C(快捷直觉也藏偏见、会误导) S2→E(逐步推理可纠错) S3→B(直觉快速识脸/察危) S4→F(费力思考耗能不能事事用) S5→D(直觉在陌生/大数问题出大错)。A/G 未匹配。

sets.passage:
[A] After many decades of relentless and damaging overfishing, a strikingly simple idea is now quietly helping the world's depleted seas to recover: that is, to set aside particular areas of ocean where all fishing is strictly banned, and then to step back and let undisturbed nature patiently do the rest of the difficult work of healing on its own. These protected zones are often called marine reserves, and the results can be remarkable.

[B] Inside these carefully protected reserves, the change over time can be genuinely dramatic and rapid. Wholly freed at last from the constant threat of the nets and the lines, the fish that live there are able to grow markedly older, considerably larger and very much more numerous than their unlucky relatives out in the surrounding open waters, which continue to be fished hard and without any real limit at all.

[C] Crucially, the great benefit of all this does not simply stay neatly put inside the lines on the map. As the protected fish populations steadily swell and become more crowded, increasing numbers of fish naturally spill out across the boundaries into the surrounding seas, where they go on to restock and replenish the very waters that the local fishermen are still entirely free to use, in a welcome process scientists call 'spillover'.

[D] Effective enforcement of these reserves, however, is genuinely and notoriously hard to achieve. The open ocean is almost unimaginably vast and, for the most part, entirely unwatched by anyone, and so a neatly drawn line on an official map means very little indeed in practice without the expensive boats, patrols and satellites actually required to monitor it and to catch those who break the rules.

[E] The ultimate success of a reserve very often hinges, in the end, on people. Marine reserves that have been thoughtfully designed together with the local fishing communities who depend on those waters, rather than simply being imposed upon them from a distant capital by unsympathetic officials, are very much more likely in practice to be understood, accepted, respected and obeyed over the long term.

[F] Yet, frustratingly, overall progress remains painfully slow. Despite the now overwhelming and widely accepted scientific evidence that such reserves genuinely work and benefit everyone in the long run, still only a very small fraction of the entire surface of the world's oceans has so far actually been granted any genuine and meaningful protection at all.

[G] The sea, it turns out, when it is only given half a chance and shown a little human restraint, reveals a truly remarkable and heartening natural power to heal and to renew itself. All that it quietly asks of us in return is the modest discipline to leave at least a few of its very richest places generously alone.
statements: As protected fish populations swell, fish spill across the boundaries and restock the surrounding waters.
Inside the reserves, freed from nets, fish grow older, larger and far more numerous.
Reserves designed together with local fishing communities are far more likely to be respected.
The ocean is vast and largely unwatched, making genuine enforcement notoriously hard.
Despite the evidence, only a small fraction of the world's oceans is so far genuinely protected.
sets.explain_zh: S1→C(鱼群增多溢出补给周边水域) S2→B(保护区内鱼更老更大更多) S3→E(与社区共建更受尊重遵守) S4→D(海域辽阔无人看守难执法) S5→F(真正受保护海域比例仍很低)。A/G 未匹配。

sets.passage:
[A] Whenever we set out to design a building, a website or an everyday tool, we almost always begin by quietly imagining a single, typical, able-bodied 'average user' who will use it. The powerful philosophy known as 'universal design' begins, deliberately and provocatively, from an altogether different starting idea: namely, that we should build, from the very outset, for the widest possible range of different human beings and abilities.

[B] Its central and rather surprising insight is that thoughtfully accommodating disability very often ends up helping absolutely everyone. The gently sloping ramps, the written captions on videos, and the large, clear signs that were originally designed and intended for some particular group with a specific need, reliably turn out, in everyday practice, to ease life enormously for parents pushing prams, for heavily laden travellers, and for the frail elderly alike.

[C] Designing in this inclusive way right from the very beginning is also, importantly, far more efficient and economical. Awkwardly adding genuine accessibility after the fact, bolting it on clumsily as an expensive afterthought or a difficult retrofit once the main work is already finished, almost always ends up costing considerably more, and working considerably less well, than simply and sensibly building it carefully in from the start.

[D] The fast-moving digital world, however, lags badly and embarrassingly behind in all this. A great many websites and smartphone apps are still, even now, routinely and thoughtlessly built in ways that quietly but completely lock out the very many people who, for whatever reason, simply cannot see a screen clearly, hear an alert, or use a conventional mouse with steady precision.

[E] Legislation has certainly pushed real change along, but only patchily and unevenly. Formal rules and laws explicitly requiring a basic level of accessibility now exist on the books in a great many countries around the world, and yet the genuine, day-to-day enforcement of those rules, and meaningful compliance with them, sadly remain weak, inconsistent and patchy almost everywhere you care to look.

[F] True and meaningful inclusion, however, goes one important step further still. The very deepest practical lesson of the whole field is that we should always strive to design genuinely with disabled people as equal partners, and not merely paternalistically for them at a distance, drawing directly and humbly on the lived expertise of exactly those who understand the real barriers best.

[G] Good, thoughtful design, in the end, is emphatically not some generous special favour grudgingly done for an unfortunate few, but rather a quiet, widespread and continual benefit to very nearly all of us. What begins as a thoughtful accommodation for the few, in other words, very often ends as a real and lasting improvement for the many, which is precisely why inclusion deserves to be treated as the rule rather than the exception.
statements: Designing genuinely with disabled people, not merely for them, is the key to real inclusion.
Ramps and captions first made for some turn out to ease everyday life for everyone.
Many websites and apps are still routinely built in ways that completely lock some users out.
Rules requiring accessibility exist in many countries, yet enforcement remains weak and patchy.
Bolting accessibility on afterward as a retrofit almost always costs more than building it in.
sets.explain_zh: S1→F(与残障者共同设计达成真包容) S2→B(为部分人做的便利惠及所有人) S3→D(网站app仍常将部分用户彻底拒之门外) S4→E(无障碍法规存在但执行薄弱参差) S5→C(事后改造通常更贵)。A/G 未匹配。

sets.passage:
[A] Roughly a full third of all the food that the world laboriously produces every year is, astonishingly, never actually eaten by anyone at all. In an age marked simultaneously by persistent hunger in many places and by mounting environmental strain almost everywhere, that single staggering figure is genuinely very hard to defend, excuse or justify on any grounds whatsoever, and it deserves far more attention than it usually gets.

[B] This vast waste of food is also, just as importantly, a serious and largely hidden environmental scandal in its own right. Every item of food that is thoughtlessly thrown away carries silently with it the whole hidden burden of all the precious fresh water, the fertile farmland, the human labour and the energy that were originally used to grow, harvest and transport it, all of it squandered and lost along with the wasted food itself.

[C] The losses, troublingly, occur at absolutely every stage all along the long and complex food chain. Crops are left to rot unharvested in muddy fields, grain spills and is spoiled in transport, fruit and vegetables decay in poor storage, and edible food is scraped, half-eaten, straight from countless plates into the bin, and all of this happens in rich and poor countries alike, though in rather different ways.

[D] The precise pattern of waste, however, differs sharply from place to place. In the wealthy, well-fed nations of the world, the great majority of the waste tends to happen late in the chain, in the supermarkets and especially in people's own homes, where perfectly good food is routinely discarded merely for looking slightly imperfect, or for quietly passing an over-cautious printed 'best before' date.

[E] Many of the most effective remedies for all this, encouragingly, are genuinely simple and cheap. The humble household habits of planning meals carefully in advance before shopping, of storing fresh food properly so that it lasts, and of deliberately using up yesterday's leftovers rather than binning them, can together cut a typical family's food waste very sharply indeed, and at almost no real cost.

[F] The mere appearance of food, meanwhile, has become a wholly needless and wasteful barrier. Vast quantities of perfectly good, nutritious and entirely edible fresh fruit and vegetables are thrown away, or else are never even put on sale in the first place, purely and simply because they happen to be slightly the wrong size, or knobbly, or amusingly and harmlessly oddly shaped.

[G] To waste a great deal less food, then, asks of us not painful sacrifice or hardship at all, but merely a little more thought and care at every single step along the way, all the way from the distant field to the dinner fork.
statements: In wealthy nations, most food waste happens late in the chain, in supermarkets and people's homes.
Vast quantities of perfectly good produce are discarded simply for being the wrong size or oddly shaped.
Food thrown away wastes all the water, land, labour and energy that went into producing it.
Simple habits like planning meals and using up leftovers can sharply cut household food waste.
Losses occur at every stage along the food chain, in rich and poor countries alike.
sets.explain_zh: S1→D(富国浪费多在零售/家庭端) S2→F(完好果蔬因尺寸形状被丢) S3→B(丢弃食物也浪费水土劳力能源) S4→E(规划/用剩菜低成本大幅减浪费) S5→C(浪费遍及供应链各环节、各国皆然)。A/G 未匹配。

sets.passage:
[A] Even today, more than a full tenth of all the people on the planet still live their entire lives without access to any reliable supply of electricity. A promising new approach, however, is now finally beginning to reach a great many of them, and tellingly it does so not through the building of vast, centralised power stations and grids, but directly through the abundant power of the sun overhead.

[B] Small, self-contained, off-grid solar power systems are now steadily bringing real electric power, for the very first time, to remote rural villages all around the developing world, lighting up homes after dark and charging mobile phones in countless places that the slow, expensive, centralised national electricity grid has simply never managed to reach at all, and very possibly never will.

[C] This nimble new model effectively leaps clean over the old, slow, expensive infrastructure altogether. In very much the same way that many poorer countries a generation ago skipped the costly business of stringing copper landline telephone wires across the land and instead jumped straight to cheap mobile phones, a great many of them are now simply skipping the enormous central electricity grid entirely and going straight to local solar.

[D] The transforming effects of this new power reach, importantly, very far beyond mere electric light. A reliable, steady local supply of electricity allows children to read and study after the sun has gone down, lets small rural clinics safely refrigerate vital vaccines and medicines, and enables tiny local businesses to extend their working hours profitably into the evening and so to grow.

[E] The single main obstacle standing in the way, predictably enough, is money. The substantial upfront cost of buying the solar panels, the batteries and the other necessary equipment is genuinely high and daunting for very poor rural households living close to the edge, and working out how to finance and spread that initial cost fairly remains the central, stubborn challenge of the whole endeavour.

[F] The long-term sustainability of these systems also depends heavily, and often overlooked, on local human skills. Off-grid solar systems clearly thrive and endure over the years in exactly those places where local people have been properly trained to maintain, troubleshoot and repair them themselves, and they tend instead to falter, break and fall silent wherever that essential practical knowledge is simply missing.

[G] Electric power, it gradually turns out, can perfectly well arrive not only as one vast, centralised grid slowly and expensively creeping outward across a country, but instead as countless small, local suns, switched on quietly one single village at a time. The future of electricity, for much of the still-unlit world, may in the end look far less like a slowly spreading web of expensive cables than like a bright scattering of these small, cheap and independent local suns.
statements: A reliable local supply of power lets children study after dark and clinics refrigerate vital medicines.
Off-grid solar systems bring electricity to remote villages the national grid has never reached.
These systems endure where local people are trained to maintain and repair them, and falter where they are not.
The high upfront cost of the equipment is the central challenge for very poor households.
Many countries are now skipping the central grid much as they once skipped landline telephones.
sets.explain_zh: S1→D(可靠电力助夜读/冷藏药) S2→B(离网太阳能给无电村供电) S3→F(本地维护技能决定可持续) S4→E(设备前期成本是核心难题) S5→C(跳过中央电网如当年跳过固话)。A/G 未匹配。

sets.passage:
[A] Could the simple act of reading a good novel quietly make you a kinder and more understanding person? A genuinely intriguing line of psychological research now seriously suggests that losing ourselves deeply in a work of fiction may, almost as a hidden side effect, quietly train and strengthen one of our most important and most distinctively human social skills.

[B] The underlying idea behind this claim is, at heart, quite simple and rather appealing. To follow a story closely and sympathetically is, in effect, to climb right inside other people's minds, to inhabit them from the inside, and to feel vividly, at least for a little while, what it must actually be like to be someone quite different from, and perhaps very unlike, our own ordinary everyday selves.

[C] A number of careful empirical studies do appear, encouragingly, to support this attractive idea directly. Committed habitual readers of serious literary fiction in particular have been found, on average, to score noticeably and consistently higher than non-readers on standard psychological tests of empathy, which is to say the basic human ability to accurately read, understand and genuinely share the felt emotions of other people.

[D] The actual mechanism behind this measurable effect may simply be repeated practice. Deeply immersed for hours in the inner life of a richly imagined fictional character, the absorbed reader is in effect constantly rehearsing the difficult mental act of vividly imagining another person's point of view from within, which happens to be the very same demanding act that genuine empathy in real, waking life always quietly requires of us.

[E] A real note of scientific caution is genuinely needed here, however. Such studies, however suggestive and appealing their findings may be, only ever reliably demonstrate a correlation, a mere statistical link, and never strict proof of cause and effect; it remains entirely possible that naturally empathetic people are simply, for that very reason, rather more drawn to reading novels in the first place.

[F] Either way, and whatever the precise direction of cause and effect finally turns out to be, the stakes involved here feel genuinely high to many. In an increasingly angry, suspicious and bitterly divided world, almost anything at all that might plausibly strengthen our fragile collective ability to imagine, and to take seriously, the inner lives of other people seems very well worth taking seriously ourselves.

[G] A story, on this hopeful and humane view, is therefore emphatically not a mere idle escape from the real world at all, but is instead something much more valuable: a quiet, private rehearsal for the demanding business of living more fully, and more kindly, within it. To open a serious novel, on this hopeful account, is to begin, quietly and quite willingly, a small and lifelong education of the sympathies.
statements: Habitual readers of serious literary fiction tend to score higher on standard tests of empathy.
To follow a story closely is to climb inside other minds and feel as someone different does.
These studies show only a correlation, not proof; empathetic people may simply read more novels.
Immersed in a fictional character, the reader repeatedly rehearses imagining another's point of view.
In a divided world, anything that strengthens our ability to imagine other lives is worth taking seriously.
sets.explain_zh: S1→C(读文学小说者共情测验得分更高) S2→B(进入故事即进入他人内心感同身受) S3→E(仅相关非因果,或共情者更爱读) S4→D(沉浸角色反复演练他人视角) S5→F(分裂世界中强化想象他人生活很重要)。A/G 未匹配。
